title: The Miles, the Metrics, and the Money
context: A cross-functional review is being held for the later Expenses capabilities that tie the module into the wider platform. Payroll owns mileage rate tables. Data wants to know what must be captured in the background. Fraud wants anomaly detection. Finance cares about where approved claims land. Product wants a deliverable roadmap that still feels coherent to customers. You are the solution architect. The General Manager is chairing.

questions.prompt: The Payroll Lead says: "We already maintain mileage schemes as rate tables in Payroll. Customers should not have to recreate those rates in Expenses just to reimburse mileage claims. The module should use what Payroll already knows." How should you respond?

options.text: I would still give Expenses its own mileage-rate setup because the module needs to stand alone. Re-entering the rates may be repetitive, but it avoids a dependency on Payroll and keeps the feature self-contained for implementation.
options.explanation: That contradicts the mileage requirement. The module is supposed to use payroll-defined mileage rate tables automatically, without requiring additional setup or configuration from customers.

options.text: That should be the design principle. The mileage feature is meant to consume the rate tables already defined in Payroll and apply them automatically inside Expenses, rather than asking customers to duplicate configuration. The architectural work is about reliable reuse of those payroll-defined rates, not rebuilding the same setup screen in another module.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it aligns directly with the requirement that Payroll remains the source of mileage rates and that customers should not need extra configuration in Expenses.

options.text: We can compromise by importing the payroll rate table once into Expenses during setup and then letting the two systems drift independently. That keeps day-one configuration light without forcing a runtime dependency between the products.
options.explanation: A one-time copy is not the same as using payroll-defined rates automatically. The requirement positions Payroll as the maintained source of the schemes, which argues against duplicating and drifting the data into Expenses.

questions.prompt: The Product Manager says: "Mileage is taking on too much complexity. For the first release, let us just accept a distance and a flat rate. Point-to-point calculation, threshold tracking, and rate changes can come later." The Payroll analyst says that without thresholds the reimbursement amounts will be wrong. How should you respond?

options.text: A flat-rate model is a valid MVP. Most employees will enter reasonable distances and the financial difference from threshold-based rates will be small enough to correct later. Shipping a simpler calculation model first lets us validate the rest of the claim flow without the mileage engine becoming the critical path.
options.explanation: A flat-rate model is not a simpler version of the same feature; it is a different feature. The mileage requirement specifies point-to-point journey capture, automatic distance calculation, accumulated mileage tracking through the year, remaining allowance visibility, and automatic rate changes when annual thresholds are crossed. Removing all of that delivers a manual input field with a multiplier, which does not match the intended value or accuracy.

options.text: The mileage feature is inherently stateful. It needs to track accumulated mileage per employee through the year, know the remaining allowance, and change the reimbursement rate automatically when a threshold is crossed. That is not optional complexity; it is the computation model that makes the reimbursement accurate. A flat-rate input would produce incorrect payments for any employee who crosses a threshold boundary, and the error would compound silently over the year.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it identifies the mileage feature as a stateful computation problem rather than a simple input-and-multiply operation. The architectural implication is that the service must maintain per-employee mileage state across the financial year and apply rate changes at the correct thresholds, which is fundamentally different from a stateless flat-rate calculation.

options.text: We should keep the simple model but add a reconciliation batch job at the end of each month that recalculates reimbursements using the correct thresholds. Employees get a flat rate at submission time and any difference is adjusted in the next pay run. That gives us the speed of a simple model with the accuracy of threshold-based rates.
options.explanation: Monthly reconciliation introduces payment corrections that confuse employees and create payroll complexity. The requirement is that the system applies the correct rate at the point of claim, not that it corrects itself later. An after-the-fact adjustment also undermines the remaining-allowance visibility the feature is supposed to provide.

questions.prompt: Customer Success says: "Home-to-work mileage deduction is edge-case territory. Can we just hard-code one behaviour globally and avoid confusing admins with another setting?" The payroll analyst points out that customer policies differ. How should you respond?

options.text: Yes. A global rule is the safest answer because it eliminates configuration errors and gives every customer the same predictable behaviour, even if their policies vary.
options.explanation: The mileage PRD explicitly says the sysadmin should be able to indicate in settings whether home-to-work mileage is deductible. A single global hard-coded behaviour would ignore that requirement.

options.text: This needs to stay configurable because the requirement specifically gives admins a setting for whether home-to-work mileage is deductible. Where that deduction applies, the system should enforce it using the employee's stored home and work addresses from People First. That is exactly the kind of policy variability the module is supposed to handle rather than flatten.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. It preserves both the configuration requirement and the enforcement mechanism that depends on People First address data.

options.text: We should remove the admin setting and let the policy-guidance assistant explain the customer's home-to-work rule instead. Employees can then self-police whether they include those miles in the claim.
options.explanation: The requirement is not just to explain the rule; it is to configure and enforce it during mileage calculation. Replacing system behaviour with advisory text would underdeliver significantly.

questions.prompt: The Data Lead says: "Anomaly detection is a future feature. We do not need to worry about background analytics now; once the module is live we can decide later what data to aggregate." Fraud disagrees. How should you respond?

options.text: That is reasonable because anomaly detection can be added after we have real usage data. It is better to avoid instrumenting too much up front and only decide what metrics matter once customers are actually using the module.
options.explanation: The analytics PRD says the data should be captured in the background specifically to drive later features such as anomaly detection, and the anomaly-detection PRD says that work is contingent on analytics being established first. Deferring the data capture would weaken that dependency chain.

options.text: We need the background analytics from the start because they are the precursor to anomaly detection, not an optional reporting extra. The PRD already names the kinds of data we should be capturing: claim counts and values over time, department-level patterns, miles travelled, number of journeys, out-of-policy submissions, out-of-policy decisions, and rejected claims. If we skip that instrumentation now, the anomaly feature arrives later with no foundation.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. It uses both PRDs together: analytics exists to create the data layer that later anomaly detection depends on.

options.text: We should start with anomaly detection rules first and then backfill analytics once we know which suspicious patterns we actually care about. The detection logic should drive the data model, not the other way around.
options.explanation: The sequencing in the requirements runs the other way. Analytics is the precursor because the system needs data to aggregate before it can evaluate meaningful anomaly patterns.

questions.prompt: The Integrations Manager says: "We should build direct connectors to external payroll and finance systems so approved claims flow through automatically. Customers expect seamless data movement, not an API they have to integrate with themselves." The Platform Architect points out the scope implications. How should you respond?

options.text: Direct connectors are the right goal. If we build and maintain integrations to the major external payroll and finance vendors, customers get a turnkey experience and we differentiate on ease of implementation. The initial investment is high but it pays back across the customer base.
options.explanation: Building and maintaining direct connectors to external vendor systems is a significant ongoing commitment that goes beyond the current scope. The requirement distinguishes between internal integration, where Expenses passes data to People First Payroll, People First Finance, and Data Explorer, and external connectivity, where connections to third-party systems are provided through API rather than as maintained native integrations.

options.text: We should distinguish between internal and external integration architectures. Internally, Expenses should pass approved claim data into People First Payroll, People First Finance, and Data Explorer through direct service integration because we own both sides. Externally, the requirement is API-based connectivity, meaning we expose well-documented APIs that external payroll and finance systems can consume, rather than building and maintaining point-to-point connectors to third-party products. That boundary matters because it defines what we own and what the customer or partner owns.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it draws a clear architectural boundary between internal service integration and external API exposure. Internal integration is tightly coupled because both services are within the platform. External connectivity is loosely coupled through APIs because the product cannot own the integration lifecycle for every third-party system.

options.text: We should avoid external connectivity entirely in the first release and keep the module self-contained. If customers need data to reach external systems, they can export it manually until we have a proper integration strategy. That keeps the scope tight and avoids committing to API contracts before we understand the real demand.
options.explanation: The requirement already includes external API connectivity as part of the feature scope. Removing it entirely would underdeliver. The right move is not to cut the capability but to describe it accurately: API-based connectivity rather than maintained native integrations.
